questions.q: What does the acronym TIFR stand for?
questions.explanation: TIFR stands for the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, a centre for the natural sciences and mathematics.

questions.q: Who founded TIFR?
questions.explanation: The physicist Homi J. Bhabha founded TIFR and served as its first director.

questions.q: In which year was TIFR established?
questions.explanation: TIFR was founded in 1945, shortly before Indian independence.

questions.q: In which city is the main campus of TIFR located?
questions.explanation: TIFR's main campus is in Mumbai (Bombay), along the Colaba seafront.

questions.q: Which industrial house provided the early philanthropic backing that gives TIFR its name?
questions.explanation: The Sir Dorabji Tata Trust provided the initial funding, and the institute carries the Tata name.

questions.q: TIFR is primarily a centre for research in which broad areas?
questions.explanation: TIFR is a leading centre for the physical, mathematical and biological sciences along with computer science.

questions.q: Homi Bhabha, the founder of TIFR, is best remembered as the father of which national programme?
questions.explanation: Homi Bhabha is regarded as the father of the Indian nuclear programme.

questions.q: TIFR is recognised as a Deemed University and functions as a National Centre of Excellence under which government department?
questions.explanation: TIFR is funded by and operates under the Department of Atomic Energy of the Government of India.

questions.q: What kind of academic institution is TIFR primarily known as?
options: A postgraduate research institute granting PhDs
questions.explanation: TIFR is a postgraduate research institution focused on doctoral research, not undergraduate teaching.

questions.q: TIFR's main building stands along the seafront of which Mumbai neighbourhood?
questions.explanation: The Mumbai campus is located at Colaba, by the Arabian Sea.

questions.q: Before settling in its present Colaba building, TIFR initially started its operations within the campus of which institution?
questions.explanation: TIFR began in 1945 in rented space at the Indian Institute of Science in Bengaluru before moving to Bombay.

questions.q: TIFR's School of Mathematics is renowned worldwide especially for its contributions to which field?
questions.explanation: The TIFR School of Mathematics earned global renown in pure mathematics, including number theory and algebraic geometry.

questions.q: TIFR served as the cradle for India's atomic energy effort and is considered the birthplace of which national agency?
options: The Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay (later BARC)
The Defence Research and Development Organisation
The Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
questions.explanation: Atomic energy research started at TIFR and led to the Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay, later renamed BARC.

questions.q: The National Centre for Biological Sciences (NCBS), a part of TIFR, is located in which city?
questions.explanation: NCBS, a centre of TIFR devoted to the biological sciences, is located in Bengaluru.

questions.q: TIFR runs a centre dedicated to interdisciplinary sciences in Hyderabad. What is it called?
options: Homi Bhabha Centre for Science Education
questions.explanation: TIFR established a campus in Hyderabad pursuing interdisciplinary research across physics, chemistry and biology.

questions.q: The Homi Bhabha Centre for Science Education (HBCSE), associated with TIFR, is best known for which activity?
options: Science and mathematics education and the Olympiad programmes
questions.explanation: HBCSE focuses on science and mathematics education and coordinates India's Science Olympiad programmes.

questions.q: TIFR operates the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT) through which of its centres?
options: Homi Bhabha Centre for Science Education
questions.explanation: The GMRT near Pune is operated by TIFR's National Centre for Radio Astrophysics (NCRA).

questions.q: The Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT), built under TIFR, is located near which city in Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: The GMRT array of dish antennas is sited at Khodad near Pune, Maharashtra.

questions.q: One of TIFR's early major achievements in the 1950s and 1960s was India's pioneering cosmic-ray research conducted in mines and balloon flights. This reflects which school of TIFR?
options: School of Technology and Computer Science
School of Natural Sciences (physics, including cosmic rays)
questions.explanation: TIFR physicists were pioneers in cosmic-ray studies, part of its School of Natural Sciences.

questions.q: One of India's earliest digital computers was built at TIFR in the 1950s. What was it called?
questions.explanation: TIFRAC (TIFR Automatic Calculator) was one of India's first indigenous digital computers, developed at TIFR.

questions.q: TIFR Centre for Applicable Mathematics (CAM), a centre of TIFR, is located in which city?
questions.explanation: The TIFR Centre for Applicable Mathematics (CAM) is based in Bengaluru.

questions.q: TIFR's lobby famously displays murals and an art collection acquired under the guidance of which founder, who was a patron of modern Indian art?
questions.explanation: Homi Bhabha, himself an accomplished artist, built TIFR's celebrated collection of modern Indian art.

questions.q: Which mathematician, long associated with TIFR's School of Mathematics, won the King Faisal International Prize for Science in 2006 (with Simon Donaldson) for work in geometry?
questions.explanation: M. S. Narasimhan of the TIFR School of Mathematics shared the 2006 King Faisal International Prize for Science with Simon Donaldson for work linking geometry and physics.

questions.q: TIFR mathematician M. S. Narasimhan is especially celebrated for a theorem (with C. S. Seshadri) on which mathematical objects?
options: Stable vector bundles on Riemann surfaces
questions.explanation: The Narasimhan-Seshadri theorem characterises stable vector bundles on compact Riemann surfaces.

questions.q: Who was appointed the first director of TIFR?
questions.explanation: Homi Bhabha, the founder, was also TIFR's first director, serving until his death in 1966.

questions.q: After Homi Bhabha's death in 1966, which physicist succeeded him as director of TIFR?
questions.explanation: M. G. K. Menon, a cosmic-ray physicist, became director of TIFR following Bhabha's death in 1966.

questions.q: The mathematician Harish-Chandra, after whom a major Indian research institute is named, was associated early in his career with which scientist who founded TIFR?
options: He was a student assistant to Homi Bhabha
He was Bhabha's first director successor
He had no connection to Bhabha
questions.explanation: Harish-Chandra worked as a research student under Homi Bhabha before turning fully to mathematics.

questions.q: TIFR led one of the deepest underground experiments in the world during the 1960s, detecting atmospheric neutrinos. Where was this experiment carried out?
options: The Kolar Gold Fields mines in Karnataka
The Jaduguda uranium mines in Jharkhand
The Neyveli lignite mines in Tamil Nadu
The Singareni coal mines in Telangana
questions.explanation: TIFR's deep-mine experiments at the Kolar Gold Fields recorded among the first atmospheric neutrino interactions in 1965.

questions.q: TIFR's main Colaba building, an architectural landmark, was designed by Helmut Bartsch of which renowned American architectural firm?
questions.explanation: TIFR's Colaba building was designed by Helmut Bartsch of the Chicago firm Holabird & Root (Holabird, Root and Burgee), commissioned by Bhabha.

questions.q: The TIFR-affiliated International Centre for Theoretical Sciences (ICTS) was established in 2007 in which city?
questions.explanation: The International Centre for Theoretical Sciences (ICTS-TIFR) was set up in Bengaluru as a centre of TIFR.
